content.text: A type alias gives a name to any TypeScript type expression. Unlike interfaces, which describe object shapes and can be reopened or extended, a type alias is a static name binding — the compiler replaces it with the aliased type wherever it appears. No runtime object is created; the name exists only in the type-checker.

Aliasing primitives makes intent visible in signatures without adding runtime overhead. For stronger safety — preventing accidental assignment between semantically different number types — you can use branded types.

An alias can name a full object shape. This is syntactically equivalent to an interface for simple cases, but type aliases are required when the shape involves mapped types, conditional types, or unions.
Type Alias vs Interface — When to Use Each
Recommended for Angular state / HTTP types
for object shapes that might be extended or merged; use
for unions, intersections, mapped types, conditional types, or tuples — anything an interface cannot express.
The most common use of type aliases is naming union and intersection types so they can be reused across the codebase without repeating the full expression.

Type aliases accept type parameters, allowing them to be reused across different concrete types. This is identical in concept to generic interfaces but required when the alias expresses a non-object type.

Mapped types transform every key of an existing type according to a rule. They can only be expressed as type aliases — not as interfaces — making the type alias keyword indispensable for building utility types.
Conditional types (`T extends U ? X : Y`) can only appear inside type aliases. They power the standard library's Extract, Exclude, NonNullable, and ReturnType utilities.
Since TypeScript 3.7, type aliases can reference themselves, enabling tree-shaped and JSON-like structures without circular-reference errors.
TypeScript ships a library of pre-built type aliases called utility types. Understanding that they are just regular type aliases demystifies them and makes it easier to write your own.
Type aliases are used throughout Angular to name complex observable, signal, and form types, keeping component and service code readable.
Type aliases can name function signatures, making callbacks, event handlers, and service method shapes reusable across a codebase.

Template literal types combined with type aliases generate sets of string types from smaller building blocks. This is useful for naming event strings, CSS classes, API routes, and Angular directive selectors.
Intersection Type Aliases — Merging and Pitfalls
Intersections combine two types so that the result satisfies both. They work cleanly for distinct object shapes but produce surprising results for overlapping primitive properties.
Complex types are easier to understand and maintain when built from smaller named aliases. Composition keeps each piece testable in isolation.

Type aliases give names to any TypeScript type expression — primitives, unions, intersections, objects, generics, mapped types, conditional types, and recursive structures. They have no runtime footprint and are essential wherever the type cannot be expressed as an interface.
Use interface for extensible object shapes; use type for everything else.
Primitive aliases add semantic clarity; branded variants add compile-time safety.
Mapped and conditional types are type-alias-only features that power the entire utility-type library.
Recursive type aliases model JSON values, trees, and route configs without
In Angular, type aliases name HTTP streams, component state machines, and form control maps.
